[16:26] philosophy that built this mentorship.
[16:31] It's taken, of course, from Maslow's Hierarchy.
[16:34] Our biggest need is essential nutrients that the body requires for normal processing and
[16:42] functions.
[16:43] When we add supplements to improve any deficits that we might have, because most of us have
[16:49] not been eating real healthy most of our lives.
[16:53] And then when we use lab work, it helps us identify those deficiencies.
[16:59] And then sometimes we need medicine, sometimes we need to quit smoking or vaping or find
[17:07] plastics or chemicals or stuff in our water.
[17:12] There are all kinds of things.
[17:13] So this sort of builds on your dietary intake, supplements, choices.
[17:18] And then we try to use the least amount of medicine for the shortest period of time.
[17:22] So people might need medicine, I'm not anti-medication, but I'm not going to go to medication first
[17:29] choice for everything, the way most mainstream providers do, the way most of us have done.
[17:35] I used to, but I've learned that that doesn't really help people.
[17:40] And so this is sort of the philosophy on which I've built this mentorship.

[17:49] Let's try going down here.
[17:52] So when we look at mainstream versus more functional practices, these are a few of the
[18:01] characteristics.
[18:02] So in mainstream medicine, they tend to write more prescriptions.
[18:05] They spend less time with patients.
[18:07] It's hard to spend time.
[18:08] They have admin and financial people over here in the background screaming at them to
[18:14] see more people to hurry things up.
[18:17] And so it's hard with all of that stuff going on in a big practice or a big clinic setting.
[18:23] It's hard to spend time with people, but that's how you're going to connect and that's
[18:27] how you're truly going to heal people.
[18:30] You've got to spend time, but it is very challenging to listen to the patient when you
[18:36] have all these other voices between guidelines and admin and your MA, your nurses, your
[18:43] other staff, your other goals or practice goals, maybe not your goals, screaming at you from
[18:51] all angles.
[18:52] In a more functional practice, we try to use less medications or at least lower doses
[18:57] of medications.
[18:59] We're more focused on food, nutrients and lifestyle.
[19:02] And most of us try to spend more time because many of us are cash-based.
[19:07] If you're insurance-based, you may find yourself in that mainstream column some of the time
[19:12] because you're really trying to do all the right things, but your time gets crunched
[19:17] pretty significantly.
[19:19] In cash-based practices, it's much easier to spend more time and the schedule improves
[19:26] over time.
[19:27] Schedule improves and then how you manage your schedule improves.
[19:31] And so that's really helpful when you think about it.
[19:34] When we look at labs, we talk about an optimal or functional lab range.
[19:40] And so that's a different number than normal in your lab reference ranges that you normally
[19:47] see printed on a lab report.
[19:50] And this picture is a little bit skewed because it's not always the middle of the
[19:55] range.
[19:56] A free T3, for instance, mid-range is barely normal.
[20:01] And so this is not always a very accurate depiction, but it was the best I could come
[20:06] up with.
[20:08] In a more optimal range, sometimes it's on the upper end.
[20:14] Every now and then it's on the lower end.
[20:17] And so it's real important that you start avoiding the word normal as much and try to
[20:22] use more optimal, more functional words.
[20:26] A really good example is vitamin D.
[20:29] During the pandemic, vitamin D got to be a little bit famous because more of our morbidity
[20:36] and mortality rates were associated with lower vitamin D levels.
[20:41] We found that below 60 created situations where patients were less likely to recover
[20:48] well, if at all.
[20:50] And so that sort of began more and more of us, even in mainstream, beginning to realize
[20:57] that vitamin D at 29 or 30 is not great or healthy.
[21:02] And so our goal is to get it closer to that 60 to 80.
[21:07] Most people freak out if they get a lab value back with a vitamin D level over 100.
[21:16] Sometimes some of the labs are now 80 max.
[21:19] And so if it's red or has a big H beside it, people freak out.
[21:25] And so don't freak out, number one, because toxicity isn't going to occur till you're
[21:31] over 200 ish.
[21:33] So the first thing you should always ask when you see a vitamin D level that's high
[21:40] is when did you take your vitamin D compared to your lab draw?
[21:44] If they just took it the last two or three days, that's why you don't need to tell
[21:47] that patient stop taking your D. It's high because they have D from the pill.
[21:54] When you are thinking about vitamin D levels, vitamin D's half-life is two full weeks.
[22:04] So if you draw a vitamin D on a patient that is currently using vitamin D most every day
[22:11] and they don't hold their vitamin D for about six weeks or so, they're going to be skewed
[22:17] a little on the high side.
[22:19] And so keep in mind that we want it to be high as a general rule.
[22:26] But don't freak out if it's over range.
[22:29] Talk to them about when they took it.
[22:30] And we'll be covering more and more of that as we go.
[22:34] This is one of the charts that kind of got real popular during the shutdown when everybody
[22:41] was getting sick and staying home and doing all this research.
[22:46] And you'll see that, well, dang it, I can't get it to do what I want it to do here.
[22:50] Let me try on my finger.
[22:53] So when you look at, if you, you can Google this chart, I've added the 30, 40, 50, and
[22:59] 60 across the top because it makes the numbers bigger for you to see.
[23:03] But you see that all of the illnesses are associated with vitamin D levels lower than
[23:12] 60.
[23:14] Some lower than 50, some lower than 40, but all less than 60.
[23:17] And so this is what we're trying to prevent.
[23:20] We're trying to prevent chronic disease.
[23:22] Did you know that there is an 83% chance of having breast cancer if your vitamin D
[23:28] level is lower than 50?
[23:33] Did you think you could prevent breast cancer just by keeping a vitamin D level?
[23:37] Vitamin D becomes all your hormones.
[23:39] Vitamin D is crucial to your immunity.
[23:43] Your whole immune system is built around vitamin D.
[23:46] So when we start looking at charts and data and statistics like this, it's important
[23:51] that we understand what it's telling us.
[23:54] But you have an 83% chance of developing breast cancer if your vitamin D level is low.
[23:59] So who's going to go start taking their vitamin D?
[24:04] Vitamin D can't work on its own.
[24:06] So one of the things we talk about as we move through mentorship is making sure
[24:10] you're getting all of the co-factors of vitamin D, not just the D.
[24:15] Many of you are, you own or work in medspas or you want to own a medspa.
[24:23] And so many of your IV hydration places and medspas will offer injectable vitamin D.
[24:29] That's not a bad moneymaker for sure, but don't depend on it.
[24:34] Use it as a stepping stone to get your patient built up a little bit.
[24:38] But then you want to continue the P.O. vitamin D, not just shots.
[24:44] Because the shots have not been shown to be sustaining a healthy vitamin D level
[24:49] long, long term.
[24:51] So we got to get it into the diet.
[24:52] And the best way to do that is with P.O. supplement.
[24:57] And so you want to make sure the patient gets mag, K2, boron, all at the same
[25:01] moment with some fats.
[25:03] Butter is your best fat.
[25:04] And we'll be talking about that more and more as we go.
[25:07] Tonight is mostly about lab optimization.
[25:09] So don't panic about any of this other information.
[25:12] It's just sort of fluff to help you understand some of the concepts
[25:15] we'll be covering as we go through this.
[25:19] Another one that we look at is iron and ferritin.
[25:23] Typically, mainstream only orders a CBC.
[25:26] But remember that hemoglobin uses ferritin.
[25:32] Ferritin becomes hemoglobin to transport oxygen.
[25:35] And so patients that report any kind of fatigue or brain fog,
[25:41] poor energy, poor stamina, palpitations, tachycardia, fainting,
[25:47] syncope, any kind of weird symptoms like that,
[25:49] you want to consider what's their ferritin.
[25:52] Get that differential driving functionally.
[25:56] Think about vitamin D. Think about iron ferritin numbers.
[26:01] Because hemoglobin is what transports our oxygen
[26:06] on the red blood cell.
[26:07] And if the body can't get oxygen, you're going to have brain fog.
[26:10] You're going to have fatigue.
[26:11] You're going to have energy drops.
[26:14] You're going to have a heart that's
[26:15] trying to compensate by pumping harder and faster.
[26:18] So you will see palpitations and various cardiac symptoms.
[26:23] Hematocrit shows us the percentage of red blood cells
[26:27] delivering oxygen when you have a really low ferritin
[26:30] or really high ferritin levels.
[26:32] You might see your patient's hematocrit fall or climb
[26:37] outside normal ranges.
[26:39] That's not healthy.
[26:40] Hematocrit and hemoglobin should
[26:42] stay in that normal range.
[26:44] But if it's outside, even if you're just doing CBC right now
[26:48] and those numbers are outside range,
[26:50] that should trigger you to wonder,
[26:52] what is their iron or ferritin number?
[26:54] And so iron and ferritin are part of our basic panel
[26:57] that we want to make sure we get on people.
[27:02] Mainstream reference range says ferritin level
[27:05] is as low as 10 to 12 normal.
[27:09] But I'm going to tell you, if you have a ferritin level below
[27:13] about 65-ish, your patient's going
[27:15] to have significant symptoms.
[27:17] And so we're going to talk about that more
[27:20] as we move through here.
[27:21] But the lower that is, the worse the patient's symptoms are.
[27:25] And if that ferritin level is 10 or 12 or less,
[27:28] it's almost life-threateningly low.
[27:31] So you don't want ferritin levels, because if they
[27:34] don't have enough ferritin, they can't
[27:36] make hemoglobin on red blood cells.
[27:39] Red blood cells will start to decline.
[27:42] The body will freak out, because not enough ferritin
[27:46] is available for red blood cell function.
[27:50] With your thyroid, we want to look at more than a TSH.
[27:55] We get a free T3, free T4, reverse T3, and a TSH
[28:01] when we're looking at thyroid balance and thyroid function.
[28:05] None of the molecules are available for energy
[28:09] production in the mitochondria except free T3.
[28:12] So then why are we drawing a TSH or free T4?
[28:17] I don't know.
[28:18] When you look at why it happened, I don't know.
[28:21] There's no real good explanation anywhere.
[28:24] Free T4 becomes free T3, so it's not
[28:28] very useful to the patient.
[28:32] TSH is a stimulating hormone.
[28:34] It doesn't tell you a whole lot.
[28:36] And so you really want to optimize free T3 and reverse T3
[28:40] to get patient symptoms better.
[28:43] What's the difference?
[28:44] Well, free T3 is the gas pedal.
[28:47] Reverse T3 is more like a brake pedal.
[28:50] So if you think about a car, I do a lot of analogies.
[28:53] You'll learn.
[28:54] Free T3 acts like your gas pedal.
[28:57] Reverse T3 acts like your brakes.
[28:58] If you want to drive to the store, to church, to school,
[29:01] to work, wherever you're going to go,
[29:04] you're going to use your gas pedal and your brake pedal
[29:07] to get you there.
[29:09] Your body has a gas pedal and a brake pedal.
[29:12] Your brake pedal drives your circadian rhythm, daytime
[29:15] versus nighttime.
[29:17] Your gas pedal drives your daytime and energy.
[29:20] And so we want to make sure your free T3 level
[29:23] is closer to four.
[29:25] So optimal is about 3.5 to four give or take.
[29:29] There are some mild outside dot range
[29:35] that can be considered OK.
[29:37] And we're going to talk about those nuances and those caveats
[29:40] as we move through our different sessions.
[29:43] Ideally, we want reverse T3 to be only about 10 or 12.
[29:47] If you don't have enough reverse T3,
[29:50] you won't have any brakes.
[29:51] You can have severely impaired sleep and rest,
[29:55] pretty significant anxiety, because there's no brakes.
[29:59] What happens if you have a car with no brakes?
[30:01] It runs fast.
[30:02] It runs downhill and picks up speed.
[30:04] And that's what your body will do
[30:06] if you don't have a braking system like reverse T3
[30:09] in place.
[30:10] If your reverse T3 is high, let's
[30:13] say it's 25 or 30.
[30:14] I've seen it as high as 50 or 60.
[30:17] If your reverse T3 is high, that means
[30:20] it's putting the brakes on with both feet.
[30:22] You're mashing the brakes all the way to the floor.
[30:23] You're pushing, pushing, pushing, pushing.
[30:25] And you're stopping, stopping, stopping.
[30:27] That's not going to feel good.
[30:28] That is more evidence of hypothyroid symptoms.
[30:32] And so that's not good.
[30:33] You never want your reverse T3 that high unexplained.
[30:37] So it's really important to watch those levels.
[30:41] And we have a whole session where
[30:44] we talk about optimizing thyroid, and then
[30:46] we refer to that often all the way through,
[30:48] because it is a fairly complex session.
[30:56] Hemoglobin A1c optimal is about 5.2 or less, 5.3 or less.
[31:03] We see diabetic complications, organ damage,
[31:07] begin about 5.3, 5.4.
[31:11] And so at 5.2 or 5.3, that's as high as we want that A1c to be.
[31:16] So that we prevent end-stage organ damage.
[31:20] These people don't even have a diabetes diagnosis.
[31:24] Think about that for just a minute.
[31:25] Ponder that just a bit.
[31:27] They have nephropathy, retinopathy, neuropathy,
[31:31] cognitive decline, but no diabetes diagnosis.
[31:37] Until their diabetes gets much, much worse.
[31:41] But the cellular damage begins very, very early.
[31:44] So this is a really good example of why,
[31:48] of how mainstream says seven or less is OK.
[31:52] And a target even for diabetes patients.
[31:55] But in a functional practice, we want
[31:59] to get that down around 5.2 or so.
[32:03] This is a chart that I have referred
[32:05] to for years and years and years.
[32:07] You can Google these charts.
[32:09] They're all over the place.
[32:12] Ideal is this 5.2 or less.
[32:17] This also lists your A1c compared to glucose
[32:22] in both milligrams per deciliter and millimoles per liter,
[32:26] which is your UK, other country kind of comparisons.
[32:33] And so you can look at this chart or Google other ones.
[32:38] But the red really startles people,
[32:40] the orange startles people.
[32:41] If you share this kind of thing on your social media
[32:44] or with your patients, it can be really helpful at helping
[32:47] them understand how dangerous it is.
[32:51] Ideal glucose is in the 80s.
[32:53] And we'd never want that glucose
[32:55] moving more than about 10 or 12 points.
[32:57] That's hard for mainstream providers to get used to.
[33:02] But it really is the clear evidence and science.
[33:07] The Diabetes Revolution is a great book
[33:09] authored by Dr. Richard Bernstein,
[33:11] who was an 89 or 90-year-old type 1 diabetic himself,
[33:17] who learned many years ago, decades ago,
[33:20] that he felt better and lived life better
[33:23] and to the fullest with his glucose in the 80s and 90s
[33:26] all the time versus any other place.
[33:29] And so he formulated what's called the law of low numbers.
[33:34] If you keep your glucose low, you need lower numbers, lower
[33:39] quantities of insulin, and you have lower episodes
[33:43] of hypoglycemia, which are life-threatening for type 1s.
[33:48] And so this is another topic that we'll
[33:53] cover quite a bit as we move through mentorship as well.
[33:56] Ideally, your postprandial glucose
[33:58] shouldn't be very different from your pre-meal.
[34:02] 10 or 12 point change is ideal.
[34:05] If it's more than that, it's time
[34:06] to figure out what drove those numbers up.
[34:09] What were the carbohydrates at that meal?
[34:14] I highly recommend a CGM.
[34:17] There are two over-the-counter brands out now.
[34:20] The Stelloe, I've been saying Stelloe.
[34:22] I learned a few months ago that it was Stelloe
[34:24] and I still can't say it right.
[34:26] And Thea are both available over-the-counter
[34:29] without a prescription.
[34:30] So really encourage your patients.
[34:32] Thea actually has a program where you as a prescriber
[34:38] can connect with them and get your patients on the CGM.
[34:43] And you can get some of the results.
[34:45] You can network with your patients and the company.
[34:49] So you may want to look into the Thea program
[34:52] if you're wanting to incorporate better glucose
[34:55] management, insulin resistance control with your patients.
[34:59] One of my favorite charts to share in mentorship
[35:02] and on social media is this one featuring Dr. Joseph Kraft
[35:09] and his research on insulin levels.
[35:11] Dr. Kraft lived up until about the 90s, early 2000s
[35:18] but he did some research back in the 20s, 30s and 40s.
[35:22] He tested 14,000 people plus glucose and insulin levels
[35:28] every hour for, as you see here, five or six hours
[35:32] and then plotted that.
[35:33] These are the results of that plotting.
[35:36] He found four basic patterns of insulin responses after food.
[35:43] And so all of them but one are considered diabetic
[35:49] because they're gonna develop diabetes.
[35:52] They have a hyper insulin response.
[35:55] And so this is one of the things that we'll continue
[35:58] to talk about and build on as we move through mentorship
[36:02] is trying to get insulin down, trying to get glucose down,
[36:06] trying to prevent diabetes and cardiovascular disease
[36:10] from happening.
[36:12] Optimal insulin is about five or less.
[36:16] Sometimes I even see problems at five.
[36:20] And what I think is happening, the more I practice
[36:23] and see this in your patients,
[36:25] mentorship participants, patients and my own
[36:29] is that sometimes the five is healthy now
[36:33] because of work the patient was doing
[36:35] before they ever came to us.
[36:38] They started reading on social media.
[36:40] They heard a TikToker, they read a book, whatever.
[36:43] They found some resources and they realized
[36:45] that they have an insulin problem.
[36:47] And so they may start fasting.
[36:49] They may go keto, carnivore, low carb, whatever.
[36:52] And they're working to improve their health.
[36:55] And so you get this level on them and it's 4.6.
[36:58] And you're like, oh, well you don't have insulin resistance
[37:01] but they still may be 50 pounds overweight or whatever.
[37:05] Yes, they still have insulin resistance.
[37:07] It's just still in the process of resolving.
[37:10] And so that's why you have to be careful
[37:14] with hard and fast numbers.
[37:15] This is about whole picture, not keying in on one number
[37:20] and saying, oh, well your insulin is fine.
[37:22] You don't have this problem.
[37:23] Yeah, it's just being reversed.
[37:27] Insulin is one of the lab results.
[37:30] There's not many that this happens to
[37:32] but insulin is one of them
[37:36] that is very, very slow to resolve 100%.
[37:41] What I mean is the number may fall.
[37:43] The patient can learn to fast.
[37:45] If they're fasting for 10 or 12, 14, 16 hours,
[37:49] their insulin's gonna fall
[37:50] and it's gonna be really, really low.
[37:52] But if they're high carb eating and overeating
[37:56] during those other six or eight hours that they're eating,
[37:59] they're still spiking really high insulin levels.
[38:01] So you can get a sort of false reading when that happens.
[38:08] And so helping patients understand that the spikes
[38:11] are also contributing to problems.
[38:14] It's just not the fasting number
[38:16] that is beneficial for us to know.
[38:18] So always evaluate your whole patient
[38:22] in light of all of this.
[38:23] Think about their intake for the last week.
[38:27] Sometimes I'll ask patients,
[38:29] tell me about what you ate last week.
[38:31] Tell me what you ate yesterday and day before.
[38:34] Just give me a brief overview
[38:36] of what typical meals look like.
[38:38] Anything to get them talking and explaining
[38:40] because they'll get to talking and telling you stuff.
[38:43] And then before you know it,
[38:44] you have learned a whole lot
[38:46] about why they're having trouble
[38:48] with some of their numbers,
[38:51] some of their lab values
[38:52] because they're just not eating in a healthy kind of way.
[38:57] And you can help them identify some of those problems.
[39:01] You can use your testosterone levels,
[39:04] osteodial levels, DHEA sulfate levels,
[39:06] thyroid numbers, triglycerides, A1C,
[39:09] and fasting blood sugar
[39:11] to help you understand insulin management.
[39:14] Even your inflammation numbers,
[39:16] your CRP, uric acid, homocysteine,
[39:20] those that are outside range really high,
[39:23] those are often because of insulin levels
[39:26] that have been pushing.
[39:28] The other thing that will happen
[39:29] that's weird with insulin
[39:31] and not many other labs
[39:32] is that if your patient had high insulin levels
[39:38] in the past or they're high now
[39:41] and they fix the insulin,
[39:44] they may not fix the testosterone,
[39:45] esterol, DHEA, thyroid,
[39:47] they may not fix all these other markers
[39:50] till way on down the road.
[39:51] I often explain fasting insulin to patients
[39:55] and the problems it causes
[39:57] kind of like a snowball rolling downhill.
[40:00] Once that snowball of high insulin starts,
[40:04] it starts a lot of processes
[40:06] that then start impacting all the way down that hill.
[40:09] And so it's gonna roll over trees,
[40:11] it's gonna push down bushes,
[40:12] it's gonna knock over people.
[40:13] So like that snowball continues to impact people
[40:18] even after that insulin level drops down to five.
[40:21] And so it's not an easy thing to fix 100%.
[40:25] So keep in mind just because their fasting insulin
[40:28] is optimal at four or five or whatever,
[40:33] they still may have evidence
[40:35] of previous insulin resistance.
[40:40] Mentioned some of the inflammation markers already.
[40:43] We want to always check inflammation markers
[40:46] because high insulin drives high inflammation.
[40:49] Plus lots of foods we eat, processed food,
[40:52] seed oils drive a lot of inflammation.
[40:55] And so always get a CRP.
[40:57] I always use a high sensitivity,
[40:59] never use a plain CRP.
[41:01] A plain CRP is gonna either tell you yes or no,
[41:04] it's high or low.
[41:05] That doesn't help you really
[41:07] because high sensitivity is gonna give you a number,
[41:10] a point like number.
[41:11] Their CRP might be 2.6, that's high.
[41:16] Even though the plain CRP says less than three is okay,
[41:20] that's healthy.
[41:21] No, it's really not.
[41:22] Anything over one, one or less is your optimal CRP level.
[41:28] And so if you're not getting an HS,
[41:31] you won't know that.
[41:33] And so make sure you're getting CRP high sensitivity.
[41:37] Another quick tip about CRP high sensitivity
[41:40] that I just learned is if your patient has a CRP level
[41:45] that's 10 or less,
[41:47] it's more likely to also be severely risky cardiovascularly.
[41:54] Cardiovascular inflammation is seen more with CRP HSs
[41:59] that are eight or 10 or less.
[42:03] Other types of inflammation,
[42:05] various autoimmune diseases, food, smoking, toxins
[42:10] will drive CRPs way high.
[42:13] But typically your cardiac CRP risk
[42:16] is with a level that's 10-ish or less.
[42:21] High, like above three or four, but in that lower range.
[42:26] White blood cells, we want to be about 6.5 to seven or less.
[42:31] Your optimal white blood count is low,
[42:34] not at the top end of the range.
[42:37] It's more toward the middle and lower end of the range.
[42:40] White blood cells are our immune system.
[42:43] If the white blood cell count is running eight or 10
[42:46] at the top of the range chronically, like all the time,
[42:49] their whole body is on alert all the time
[42:53] because of inflammation.
[42:56] The whole army is coming to work every day,
[42:59] seven days a week, 365, it never gets a break.
[43:03] And so that's not healthy for the bone marrow,
[43:06] it's not healthy for the person.
[43:08] So watch for the white blood cell count very carefully.
[43:12] And if it's higher than about 6.5 or seven,
[43:16] you can talk to your patient
[43:17] about risks for inflammatory processes.
[43:22] Homocysteine can be a marker of inflammation.
[43:25] It can also be a marker for an MTHFR variant.
[43:29] We'll talk about MTHFR variants genetically
[43:35] in another couple of sessions.
[43:37] We'll talk about it some with thyroid,
[43:39] we'll talk about it some when we do inflammation.
[43:42] But MTHFR is a genetic variant that alters
[43:47] a lot of problems, so methylation.
[44:02] So remember methylation is a process
[44:04] where chemicals are separated during processing,
[44:09] during digestion, absorption.
[44:12] And so methylation is a process of cleaving
[44:15] two ingredients.
[44:18] And so a really easy example is B12.
[44:22] It's the most famous one.
[44:23] But if you take a B12 called cyan cobalamin,
[44:28] which is the typical cheapest form of B12
[44:30] on the market today,
[44:32] and you have one of these variants
[44:34] that won't separate appropriately,
[44:39] you take cyan cobalamin,
[44:41] it goes through your system unused, you get rid of it.
[44:43] So that B12 was never broken apart
[44:46] from the cyanide molecule,
[44:47] it was never able to be absorbed.
[44:49] And so we talk about that some more
[44:51] in this program as well.
[44:54] Uric acid, most of us are very familiar
[44:57] with testing uric acid for our gout patients
[44:59] and sometimes our kidney patients.
[45:01] But uric acid is the newest inflammation marker.
[45:05] If you follow my page,
[45:07] you may have seen me post
[45:08] about Dr. David Perlmutter sometimes.
[45:11] He has a new book out soon,
[45:14] I think maybe in August.
[45:16] His most recent book came out two or three years ago
[45:19] called Drop Acid.
[45:21] And it is sort of a review of the evidence
[45:24] relating uric acid to cardiovascular
[45:26] and other inflammatory processes.
[45:29] So we want uric acid to be five or less,
[45:33] not six, like we say for gout.
[45:36] We want all uric acid levels to be less than five.
[45:39] That poses the least risk for kidneys,
[45:41] for gout and the heart.
[45:45] Ferritin is not always an inflammation marker,
[45:48] it's an iron marker.
[45:49] Ferritin becomes hemoglobin on the red blood cell
[45:51] that transports oxygen all around the body cells.
[45:54] But if ferritin is really, really high,
[45:57] it becomes an inflammation marker.
[46:00] It is an acute phase reactant,
[46:02] really high stress, really high inflammation,
[46:04] some wacky thing going on in your life
[46:07] can really trigger an over storage of iron.
[46:11] Just shunts iron right out of iron into storage
[46:15] and that will create that really, really high level.
[46:19] TPO and thyroglobulin antibodies
[46:22] are your two major Hashimoto's markers.
[46:24] You always want to test those on your initial labs
[46:29] when you are working up fatigue.
[46:32] So that means for a new workup,
[46:35] you're gonna get a TSH, free T3, a free T4,
[46:42] a reverse T3, a TPO thyroid peroxidase antibodies
[46:48] and a thyroglobulin antibody.
[46:50] So you're gonna get six thyroid levels
[46:52] on every new patient because of fatigue, okay?
[46:58] And so these tell you if the patient
[47:01] likely has Hashimoto's.
[47:03] Technically, I do use the normal ranges
[47:07] for TPO and thyroglobulin antibodies,
[47:09] but if your patient has known Hashimoto's
[47:13] and they've had markers in the hundreds of thousands before
[47:18] and they get to less than a hundred,
[47:19] they are doing great.
[47:21] So don't keep like after them to get better.
[47:24] Getting the TPO and thyroglobulin antibodies
[47:27] down to less than a hundred is a great feat
[47:30] and should be rewarded
[47:32] because normally Hashimoto's is a very difficult to control.
[47:37] There are other autoimmune markers, I don't test them
[47:40] unless there are known specific symptoms that correlate
[47:43] and we'll cover some of those in our inflammation setting.
[47:47] Like for instance, if you have a patient
[47:48] sitting in front of you that has a butterfly rash,
[47:51] don't you want to order some lupus workup?
[47:55] So in that case, yes, I would add lupus
[47:57] to my labs that I'm doing.
[47:59] So it really depends on what else you may want to test.
[48:04] I just finished building a long haul COVID course
[48:09] for the elite NP and we have a small session
[48:13] that we'll cover in mentorship,
[48:16] but if your patient's sitting there telling you
[48:18] they think they have long COVID
[48:19] and they have all these symptoms
[48:21] and they've been sick forever,
[48:22] add a spike protein, another autoimmune marker
[48:26] to your lab values.
[48:27] You can order whatever you want that you can justify.
[48:32] So keep that in mind.
[48:37] Electrolytes, when you're looking at your CMP,
[48:39] there's a few things we look at
[48:41] and electrolytes is one of them.
[48:43] You want sodium to be at the upper end of the range,
[48:45] bare minimum 140.
[48:47] I aim for about 141, 142.
[48:51] I like for potassium to also be
[48:53] in the upper half of the range.
[48:55] Most of us were told back in our own school
[48:57] that our elders always had to be at four or better.
[49:00] Well, why just elders?
[49:02] I find that people that have tons of muscle cramps,
[49:05] Charlie horses, toes drawing,
[49:08] those kinds of things are typically very, very low
[49:10] in their sodium levels.
[49:12] So get people on salt.
[49:14] And yes, I know some of you just had a heart attack
[49:16] because I said, get people on salt.
[49:18] We're gonna talk more about salt
[49:20] as we move through this,
[49:21] but salt is not the villain of blood pressure
[49:24] or hypertension or health problems at all.
[49:29] The body absolutely must have salt.
[49:31] Remember your sodium and potassium pump from biology.
[49:35] If you don't take a look at that, look that back up.
[49:38] Remember that for every muscle contraction,
[49:40] sodium and potassium have to cross the cell membrane
[49:43] to create that action potential.
[49:46] If you don't have enough sodium,
[49:48] muscles don't function right.
[49:50] The number one cause that I find
[49:52] for fibromyalgia and chronic fatigue syndrome
[49:55] is poor salt intake.
[49:56] Those patients can add their salt
[49:58] and their symptoms literally start to disappear within days.
[50:02] So it really is an amazing thing to watch.
[50:08] Some of your patients are taking magnesium, chromium,
[50:12] selenium, zinc, copper randomly and without direction.
[50:18] Be very, very careful with those.
[50:21] I never recommend heavy minerals or heavy metals
[50:26] or those kinds of ingredients without lab values.
[50:30] Now, magnesium gets used up a lot.
[50:32] And if you take a lot of it,
[50:33] the bones will communicate with the bloodstream
[50:36] and it'll just be sucked up into the bone tissue.
[50:39] So it's not a big deal for a mag,
[50:40] but all of your other heavy metals,
[50:43] most of your trice minerals
[50:44] should not be taken willy-nilly.
[50:46] Those need to be tested.
[50:49] Always get a red blood cell version
[50:51] if you're gonna draw magnesium, chromium,
[50:53] selenium, iron, copper, molybdenum,
[50:57] all of those trace minerals need to be drawn
[51:01] using the red blood cell version
[51:03] because it's more stable.
[51:05] It's not as impacted by food and digestion and fluctuations.
[51:13] Continuing to look at your CMP,
[51:16] you want your liver enzymes to be evaluated well,
[51:20] alkaline phosphatase.
[51:22] Ideally, we want around 60.
[51:25] It can be 55, it can be 65,
[51:27] but kind of outside that 60-ish range,
[51:31] we really don't want alkaline phosphates to stay there.
[51:34] If your liver enzymes are lower,
[51:37] you can always count on some mild malnutrition.
[51:41] Look at their proteins, their vitamin D,
[51:43] their iron, their iodine.
[51:44] Look at all of those things together as a whole picture
[51:48] and you'll start to see the mild malnutrition.
[51:51] Sometimes it's pretty moderate to severe malnutrition,
[51:54] but you'll see the liver enzymes
[51:57] support your thought process that,
[52:00] you've already told your patient
[52:01] they're low in their proteins,
[52:02] they're low in their D, they're low in their iron,
[52:04] and now we see that that's true
[52:06] because now we have liver enzymes
[52:08] that don't even get up to optimal,
[52:11] and so they are at the bottom end of the range.
[52:13] AST and ALT, you want right around 20,
[52:16] so usually about 16 to 25 is good for your AST and ALT,
[52:22] but if it's lower than that,
[52:24] consider the malnutrition.
[52:26] If it's higher than that,
[52:28] with any of your three liver enzymes,
[52:30] consider liver stress.
[52:32] Think about alcohol, medications, Tylenol.
[52:36] What did they do the last few days before the draw?
[52:39] Did they have a severe migraine
[52:41] and they took a bone load of meds?
[52:44] Did they have a wedding they went to over the weekend
[52:47] and they drank a lot over the weekend,
[52:50] and so their liver enzymes are elevated
[52:51] because their body was metabolizing all the alcohol?
[52:55] Look at the whole story, ask patients,
[52:57] why do you think your liver enzymes were elevated?
[52:59] These are the things that might contribute.
[53:03] Do any of those things connect for you?
[53:07] And so oftentimes the patients will say,
[53:09] oh yeah, we did this and this and this,
[53:11] and you'll be like, okay,
[53:12] well, we'll just retest your liver enzymes,
[53:14] your CMP next time and we'll see if they come on down,
[53:18] and if they don't, we'll have to figure that out.
[53:21] Keep in mind that fatty liver disease is quite common
[53:24] with all the metabolic dysfunction in America today,
[53:27] around the world today,
[53:29] and so liver enzymes can indicate
[53:32] some possible fatty liver disease.
[53:34] Keep in mind, however,
[53:36] that fatty liver disease is diagnosed
[53:38] by the gold standard of imaging, not labs.
[53:41] So don't ever tell a patient,
[53:42] you have fatty liver based only on lab work,
[53:45] and if they come to you already being told that,
[53:49] teach them and retrain them to understand
[53:52] that fatty liver is diagnosed by imaging, not by labs.
[53:59] This one's a biggie,
[54:00] and this one gets a lot of medical professionals
[54:04] all stirred up and confused, but it's really simple.
[54:08] So on your CMP, you get a protein level
[54:11] and an albumin level.
[54:13] Sometimes you'll get a globulin level, but not always.
[54:20] The math problem is globulin plus albumin equals protein.
[54:28] So if you have two of the three levels,
[54:31] you can calculate the third one.
[54:34] So if you only have a protein level and an albumin level,
[54:37] you wanna say seven minus four is three, okay?
[54:41] So globulin would be three.
[54:47] Remember that saying from probably high school biology,
[54:51] proteins are the building blocks of the cell.
[54:53] Remember that?
[54:56] That's your globulin.
[54:57] Globulins are the building blocks of the cell.
[55:00] So globulins become immune, immunoglobulin system.
[55:06] Globulins become hormones, globulins become everything.
[55:11] Skin cells, bone cells, muscle cells, brain cells,
[55:14] every last thing that we have that make us up
[55:18] starts out as a globulin kind of protein.
[55:22] So if your globulin is low,
[55:25] none of your body systems
[55:27] are going to be effectively working.
[55:30] The number one call,
[55:32] somebody asked this question a minute ago,
[55:33] the number one cause of low white blood cell count
[55:36] is poor globulin, a globulin that is low.
[55:40] If the globulin is low,
[55:42] there's not enough protein to build new cells
[55:46] and something must be sacrificed.
[55:48] And for a lot of patients,
[55:50] that winds up being the white blood cell.
[55:54] So watch for that.
[55:55] If you have somebody that's having low white counts,
[55:58] when you get labs, look at their proteins.
[56:02] If their globulin level is a little low-ish, that's why.
[56:08] We don't want globulins to be low.
[56:10] We don't want globulins to be high, right around three,
[56:13] like 2.9 to 3.2 is your ideal,
[56:16] perfectly right in the middle of the range.
[56:19] We don't want low or high.
[56:23] Low is poor protein intake or absorption.
[56:26] We don't know which one it is
[56:29] until we talk to a patient, collect some history
[56:31] and kind of figure out what's going on.
[56:32] Remember the whole picture.
[56:39] And then high globulin is almost always
[56:48] from low protein.
[56:50] I know, that's weird.
[56:51] So think about what happens
[56:53] if we are not consuming enough protein
[56:56] for the body to get amino acids,
[56:57] what's the body gonna start to do at some point?
[57:00] Anybody wanna guess?
[57:03] Type it in the chat or you can unmute and just chime in.
[57:07] If there's not enough protein intake or absorption,
[57:11] what's the body gonna start doing?
[57:17] Eat muscle.
[57:17] Amy and Stephanie, you're exactly right.
[57:19] It's gonna start breaking down your own muscle.
[57:22] That's protein and it's accessible.
[57:25] And so it's gonna start breaking down your muscle
[57:27] to get amino acids to do the jobs that the body needs done.
[57:31] There's not a real good break on that system.
[57:35] Once that process starts, it just breaks down a lot.
[57:39] And there's not a real good way to like stop it.
[57:43] And so a lot of times you'll find really high albumin
[57:47] or globulin levels because that's what's happening.
[57:50] And so if you see really high albumin or globulin,
[57:53] that should be your first thought.
[57:55] Maybe you're not getting enough protein in your diet.
[57:58] Your body's breaking down muscle
[57:59] and it's just getting too much of it.
[58:01] So that kind of needs to become part of your thought process
[58:05] when you see these numbers.
[58:07] And that applies to albumin or globulin.
[58:09] If you have one of them low, one of them high,
[58:12] always consider that something is up with protein,
[58:16] either poor intake or poor absorption of the protein.
[58:22] Remember that albumin is responsible mostly
[58:24] for water balance inside and outside the cell.
[58:27] It keeps your fingers healthy until you get in plain water
[58:32] and then it sucks the salt and albumin right out
[58:35] of your cell and the cells begin to collapse.
[58:38] And that's what gives you pruning wrinkly fingers
[58:40] when you sit in the tub for a long time.
[58:43] So keep an eye on your albumin and your globulin.
[58:48] When we talk about hormones,
[58:52] we're gonna do a lot with hormones.
[58:55] We're gonna talk about hormones a lot during mentorship.
[58:58] DHEA sulfate is your precursor for everything.
[59:03] We're gonna talk about a lot of ingredients
[59:08] that make hormones, but early on,
[59:12] one of the first molecules is DHEA sulfate.
[59:14] And so we want DHEA sulfate, again,
[59:18] to be right in the middle.
[59:19] Not very high, not very low.
[59:21] If it's high, we're going to wonder
[59:24] about insulin resistance.
[59:25] If it's low, we're gonna wonder about insulin resistance,
[59:29] poor protein intake, poor nutrition,
[59:31] like all the things we've been mentioning so far.
[59:34] So keep in mind too that DHEA sulfate varies
[59:38] based on age and gender.
[59:40] Men have a normal range up to about 420, 450.
[59:45] Post-menopausal women only goes to about 220 or 230.
[59:50] So watch your range and be careful.
[59:54] Don't just settle on a number.
[59:55] When I'm doing my lab worksheet
[59:57] that I showed y'all early in the session,
[59:59] I will write my DHEA sulfate number.
[60:02] Let's say it's 214 and I'll slash 230
[60:06] so that I know this menopausal lady is 211
[60:12] out of a total of 230.
[60:13] So the highest level is the 230 and her level is the 211.
[60:17] That way I know when I'm looking briefly at the report
[60:20] I've written out in my appointment,
[60:22] oh, your DHEA is low on the high side
[60:25] because it's at the very top of the range.
[60:28] We need to get that down a little bit.
[60:30] And so then I don't have to wonder,
[60:31] oh, what age is she?
[60:32] Was this a male?
[60:33] Like I don't have to wonder,
[60:34] I have both those numbers right in front of me and handy.
[60:40] Ideally for total testosterone for men and women,
[60:44] we want it mid-range or higher.
[60:46] The women level is beginning to change a little bit.
[60:50] We're wanting more testosterone for women to feel better
[60:55] and we're starting to see some of those levels go
[60:58] above range, free testosterone.
[61:02] We want to be the upper third of the range.
[61:05] And then we also want not just numbers, but we compare.
[61:10] So if you have a total testosterone on a female
[61:14] who is running a 50-ish, 45 or 50 for her total T,
[61:19] we should expect most women at certain points of their cycle
[61:27] to run an estradiol level around that 50.
[61:30] So you're wanting a ratio that has that total T
[61:33] and the estradiol level pretty close together
[61:38] and not far apart.
[61:39] If their estradiol level is 320
[61:43] and their testosterone is 10,
[61:45] they have some severe insulin resistance
[61:48] and estrogen dominance going on.
[61:50] And so that needs to be addressed.
[61:52] We also want estrone to be as low as possible.
[61:57] Does anybody know the difference
[61:58] between estradiol and estrone?
[62:00] Does anybody want to share the differences?
[62:02] If you want to type it, you can,
[62:03] or you can unmute and chime in.
[62:08] Anybody want to share the difference
[62:09] between estradiol and estrone?
[62:12] I'm not trying to put anybody on spot.
[62:13] Just if you know, you can say, and if you don't,
[62:16] I'm gonna tell you, I'm not gonna put anybody on spot.
[62:22] Somebody answered in the chat.
[62:24] Let's see.
[62:28] Lisa says, estrone is harmful, sort of.
[62:32] Yeah, Amy, you're right.
[62:33] Estrone is the estrogen of menopause.
[62:36] We don't want estrone to be higher.
[62:40] Yes, Angela, it usually is.
[62:41] We don't want it to be higher.
[62:43] Because when estrone is higher,
[62:46] patients have more symptoms.
[62:49] A lot of times I have patients describe
[62:52] their menopausal symptoms as sort of like this.
[62:56] I feel like I'm dragging around
[62:59] an 80-pound suitcase with me everywhere I go.
[63:02] And so I get a clear image of that.
[63:04] I'm dragging something that's heavy, a burden.
[63:08] I don't feel well.
[63:09] I'm tired.
[63:10] And so estrone is like that.
[63:12] Estrone drives a lot of menopausal symptoms.
[63:16] Estrone will drive a lot of the brain fog,
[63:19] a lot of the irritability, the mood swings,
[63:22] the poor sleep patterns.
[63:26] So if your patient's complaining
[63:28] of menopausal type vasomotor symptoms,
[63:32] consider their estrone is probably much too high.
[63:35] And we want it to be lower than estradiol.
[63:38] Normally, without any assistance,
[63:40] estrone will be higher in menopause.
[63:43] Our goal, though, as functional practitioners,
[63:46] is we will be able to get that down.
[63:48] We can do things to drive estrone down
[63:51] and improve estradiol levels and raise that up.
[63:57] Yeah, everybody always asks that, Lisa.
[63:59] There's not a level number that we can look at
[64:03] because it's driven by the estradiol number.
[64:06] So if estradiol is 60 and estrone is 70,
[64:11] that's a bad number.
[64:12] But if estrone is 70 and estradiol is 110,
[64:16] that's not a bad number.
[64:18] It's about the relationship.
[64:20] So that's why this mentorship is really helpful
[64:25] for providers and practitioners
[64:28] because it helps you start to look at things
[64:31] a little differently, not just the flat number.
[64:33] Oh, I have to do this because this number is low or high.
[64:37] No, it's about beginning to understand
[64:40] that all of these are connected.
[64:42] All of this chemistry interacts with one another
[64:45] and learning the relationships that drive better symptoms.
[64:50] So estradiol is your youthful estrogen of childbearing age.
[64:56] It's what thickens the lining of your uterus
[64:59] to have a period every month.
[65:01] Estradiol is the bioidentical prescription.
[65:06] We give women to drive that estrone down
[65:09] and to give them better estradiol levels
[65:11] to have better symptoms.
[65:14] Thus, why we typically want patients to have a mammogram
[65:20] and or a uterine sonogram
[65:22] early in their estradiol support program.
[65:26] And so those are all things to think about.
[65:29] The majority of your hormone imbalances
[65:31] are gonna be driven by insulin and or malnutrition.
[65:36] Insulin plays the biggest role.
[65:41] So there's a boatload of references in here.

[75:29] The other thing that I haven't mentioned yet
[75:32] with regard to being off of things is, remember biotin.
[75:36] It's a great B vitamin.
[75:38] It's very helpful.
[75:39] Great for hair, skin, and nails.
[75:41] It's in a lot of supplements out there,
[75:44] not just hair, skin, and nail stuff.
[75:46] It's in a lot of collagen, protein powders, energy drinks.
[75:49] It's in a lot of stuff,
[75:52] but biotin will skew lots of your lab results.
[75:55] University of Iowa has a PDF out
[75:58] that tells you which things it goes up and down.
[76:02] It'll skew your results up or down.
[76:05] You can Google University of Iowa biotin,
[76:08] and it pops right up.
[76:10] It's a two or three page document,
[76:12] and it recommends, I think, a day or two off of biotin,
[76:15] but I tell people a week, and this is why.
[76:20] Some of these people are self-dosing
[76:23] because they're literally devastated and desperate.
[76:27] They have looked for anything and everything
[76:31] that they can find to make them feel better,
[76:33] make their hair grow, make their skin better.
[76:35] Whatever their problem is,
[76:37] they're struggling to find something to work,
[76:41] and so they just keep adding these supplements.
[76:42] Well, they might be on three or four things
[76:44] that have biotin in them,
[76:47] and so they may not know it
[76:49] because they're not taking a biotin pill.
[76:52] They don't know it's in their hair, skin, and nail thing.
[76:55] They don't know it's in their vitamin D pill
[76:57] or their B complex or their multivitamin.
[77:01] These people come to me,
[77:02] I've had people come to me taking four or five
[77:05] of the same ingredients labeled a different title.
[77:09] They don't know, so we have to remind them
[77:14] it's about the actual ingredients on the back of the label.
[77:17] You got to look for biotin and everything
[77:19] and make sure you stop that a week ahead of time
[77:22] so that all of the biotin can have time to detox.
[77:27] Optimal B12 and tholite,
[77:28] I don't really have optimals, amethyst.
[77:30] That's another one of those whole picture things.
[77:33] So, optimal B12 is we want it
[77:37] to be toward the top of the range,
[77:38] but the only time you're gonna see it
[77:40] at the top of the range is if they're supplementing
[77:42] or eating exactly right all the time, okay?
[77:46] So, that means they're always gonna have B12 levels
[77:51] for the bottom end of the range or low.
[77:54] So, I look at the B12 and it's on my list,
[77:57] it's on the lab worksheet,
[77:58] but I really don't have a set number.
[78:01] If your folate is low or above range,
[78:06] they have a B12 deficiency.
[78:10] So, keep that in mind.
[78:11] We want folate mostly kind of in the middle,
[78:14] not real low, not real high.
[78:17] The other problem amethyst with B12 levels
[78:20] is that varied levels, especially the higher ones,
[78:26] might be an indication of that MTHFR variant
[78:30] we mentioned earlier.
[78:31] We're gonna talk about that some more,
[78:33] but MTHFR variants drive poor B12 metabolism.
[78:39] And so, somebody might be on three or four B12 supplements,
[78:44] and their level might be greater than 2,000.
[78:46] I've had it greater than 4,000 before
[78:48] on people really, really high,
[78:50] and they still feel like crap,
[78:52] and they have all the symptoms of low B12,
[78:55] because they're not able to separate cyan cobalamin apart,
[79:00] like that molecule is staying stuck together.
[79:02] So, their body is not able to access the B12.
[79:05] It's all in their bloodstream,
[79:07] but it's not accessible, not usable by their body.
[79:10] So, if you have an MTHFR or COMT genetic variant
[79:14] that causes methylation defects,
[79:17] they can't use any of that B12.
[79:19] So, that's why there's not really a set number for B12.
[79:22] You gotta look at the whole picture.
[79:23] Are they on a B12 supplement?
[79:25] Is it in their protein shake?
[79:27] Are they drinking an Anise every day?
[79:29] Like, what's going on
[79:31] that's driving that B12 level to be high?
[79:35] And if they're not getting any B12,
[79:39] if their B12 is low,
[79:40] are they not eating any red meat?
[79:42] Because that's the big source of B12.
[79:45] So, there's not really a number,
[79:47] but those are the guides.
[79:48] We kinda want it to be in the middle
[79:50] to upper end of the range of B12,
[79:52] but we don't want it low,
[79:54] and we definitely don't want it above range.
[79:57] The one caveat that you might wanna make a note of
[80:00] and keep in mind is if you consistently
[80:03] have a B12 level that's above range,
[80:05] even when the patient swears
[80:07] they are not using anything to raise B12,
[80:11] you might wanna consider a solid tumor.
[80:14] Some of your various tumors, brain, liver, bone,
[80:19] some of those cancers can overproduce B12.
[80:23] So, it's not common.
[80:24] I've never found it to be true with any of my patients,
[80:28] and I have seen hundreds of thousands of patients.
[80:32] So, it is rare,
[80:34] but you need to keep that kinda little niggling
[80:36] in the back of your mind,
[80:37] going, hmm, hmm, like,
[80:41] to keep an eye out for that.
[80:43] So, what I usually do is if it's really high the first time,
[80:46] we talk about all of the things
[80:49] that might be driving it up,
[80:50] and so the patient then goes and reads labels
[80:53] and starts looking and trying to figure it out,
[80:54] and then the next time we draw, it usually is down.
[80:59] Another thing that I will sometimes use
[81:01] is a specific product called B minus.
[81:04] Seeking Health makes a great product.
[81:07] It's the B vitamins, but no B12.
[81:10] So, for people that are struggling
[81:12] with weirdly elevated B12 levels
[81:17] or stays on the upper end,
[81:19] you can put them on Seeking Health's B minus product
[81:22] for your next three, four, six months,
[81:25] and when they come back,
[81:26] their B12 often falls and is way back down
[81:30] into a good range.
[81:34] Kidela, you're seeing them for consult first
[81:37] and holding cell, yes, absolutely.
[81:40] So, I know a lot of people in the functional space
[81:43] offer like a 15-minute consult where they order the labs.
[81:46] If you're running a cash practice, you can do that
[81:49] because you don't have to justify any of your labs.
[81:53] My practice is a hybrid practice.
[81:55] I have some insurance patients
[81:57] and some cash pay patients.
[82:00] So, I treat them all the same.
[82:02] So, we have the same workflow for everybody,
[82:04] and it's not confusing for any of the staff,
[82:07] but we put new patients on schedule.
[82:10] I spend an hour with them collecting my history.
[82:15] I do some, I use some questionnaires
[82:18] that are shared in the Optimontra system.
[82:20] If that's what you have, you're welcome to find them.
[82:24] All of my Optimontra forms are,
[82:27] they include JHHC in the title.
[82:30] So, you can go to the Optimontra library
[82:33] and search JHHC, and you will find my forms.
[82:37] It's not, but three or four, maybe five of them.
[82:41] And then we get those questionnaires sent out
[82:44] to patients before their appointment.
[82:46] So, I see them completed when I see the patients.
[82:50] So, I'm able to open up that completed questionnaire,
[82:53] go through those questions,
[82:55] asking them to tell me more about these symptoms
[82:58] and that kind of stuff.
[82:59] And it can be really helpful
[83:02] if patients are answering questions before you see them.
[83:04] They'll often answer questions on a piece of paper,
[83:07] way more openly and honestly than they will
[83:10] if you ask them in person.
[83:11] Plus, it gives you a chance to,
[83:15] let's say they answered their fatigues question
[83:19] on the thyroid questionnaire.
[83:21] There's a question about, all right,
[83:22] do you wake tired or are you always tired?
[83:25] Are you always tired, 10.
[83:26] You wake tired, two.
[83:28] Okay, so you mentioned on your questionnaire,
[83:32] you have a lot of fatigue.
[83:33] Tell me a little bit about your energy.
[83:37] When do you get tired?
[83:39] What happens when you get tired?
[83:41] Are you missing out on life?
[83:43] Like, I try to get a lot of questions thrown out there
[83:46] so that they're not just a, are you tired?
[83:51] No?
[83:53] Well, yeah, they are, they wouldn't be there.
[83:55] They just don't know the right words.
[83:57] And so you have to get them to tell me,
[84:01] tell you things like, tell me about your fatigue.
[84:04] You said you have some, you're tired sometimes
[84:06] when you wake up, tell me what that's like.
[84:08] Is it because you didn't sleep well?
[84:11] Like, get them to talking because yes and no questions
[84:15] are the absolute worst questions
[84:17] for a functional practitioner.
[84:21] Absolutely, my time is my expertise
[84:25] and that's worth money, Kadella.
[84:28] So I charge them up front for the hour long visit
[84:30] to collect information.
[84:32] They wait a week or two, go get their labs.
[84:34] It's two more weeks or longer before we get results back.
[84:38] Another tip you should be aware of,
[84:40] the reverse T3 is gonna take two weeks to come back.
[84:43] The reverse T3 takes a long, long time.
[84:45] And so you might get part of your results
[84:48] within two or three days and ready to schedule
[84:50] that patient back.
[84:51] Do not do it.
[84:52] Do not do it.
[84:55] Give yourself time to make sure the reverse T3 is back
[84:59] and set an alert in your Opto mantra, your Athena,
[85:04] whatever you're doing, whatever system you're using,
[85:06] set an alert, some kind of way to check
[85:09] for that reverse T3 in about 10 more days
[85:11] because it's gonna take two full weeks for that to come back
[85:14] and you cannot do an adequate chart review, lab review
[85:18] without the reverse T3.
[85:19] It is part of this whole picture.
[85:21] Don't do it.
[85:22] If the lab didn't run a test,
[85:25] if they forgot the insulin,
[85:27] some of your lab texts don't know the difference
[85:29] between insulin and A1C and they think it's the same thing.
[85:33] They won't draw a reverse T3
[85:34] because they've never heard of that.
[85:36] Like there's a few times that the lab texts just screw up
[85:40] one way or the other, don't panic.
[85:41] Just do the best you can with what you have.
